4-1.v: Cost Behavior: Fixed, Variable & Mixed Costs
Costs behave differently as activity levels change. Understanding how each type behaves — in total and per unit — is fundamental to all of cost accounting and exam questions.

r: Stays constant within the relevant range — does NOT change as production increases or decreases
Decreases as production increases; increases as production decreases
Increases as production increases; decreases as production decreases
Stays CONSTANT — per-unit variable cost does not change with activity
Has both a fixed base amount plus a variable component
Changes with activity but never reaches zero

4-1.v: Exam trap: If production doubles, total fixed cost stays the same but fixed cost PER UNIT is cut in half. Variable cost PER UNIT stays the same but TOTAL variable cost doubles.
Fixed Costs & the Relevant Range

Fixed costs do not change in total as long as activity stays within the relevant range. The relevant range is the band of activity within which fixed cost assumptions hold. If activity exceeds capacity, the company must add facilities — jumping to a new relevant range with higher total fixed costs.
Over a long enough period, ALL costs behave like variable costs. In the short run, treat fixed costs as fixed within the relevant range unless the question tells you otherwise.
Variable Costs

Variable costs are incurred only when production occurs. Direct materials and direct labor are the classic examples. As production increases, total variable costs increase proportionally, but cost per unit remains unchanged. The selling price minus all unit variable costs equals the unit contribution margin.
Mixed Costs: Semi-Variable vs Semi-Fixed

A semi-variable cost (also called a mixed cost) has a fixed base plus a variable element. Example: a utility bill with a fixed monthly fee plus a charge per kilowatt used. A semi-fixed cost (step cost) is fixed over a small range but jumps suddenly when activity crosses a threshold. Example: one nurse per 25 patients — each time patient load increases by 25, one new nurse is hired, causing a step increase in total cost.

v: Semi-variable: smooth upward slope — starts at a fixed base and rises linearly
Semi-fixed (step cost): flat for a range, then jumps, then flat again — like staircase steps
Key difference: semi-variable starts at a base and moves upward smoothly; semi-fixed moves upward in steps

4-1.v: 3 Cost Measurement Methods
When allocating costs to products, companies use one of three systems:

r: Applied at STANDARD rate × STANDARD quantity allowed for actual output
Predetermined rate × standard quantity ALLOWED for actual output
Applied at ACTUAL rate × ACTUAL quantity used
Predetermined rate × ACTUAL quantity USED in production
Applied at ACTUAL rate × ACTUAL quantity used
Actual OH rate × actual quantity used (seldom used — causes cost fluctuations)

4-1.v: The key distinction: Standard costing uses ALLOWED quantity (what should have been used for actual output). Normal costing uses ACTUAL quantity used. Both use a predetermined overhead rate for OH. Only actual costing uses actual rates for everything.
Predetermined Overhead Application Rate
Because actual overhead may not be known until after period-end, companies calculate a predetermined rate at the start of the year using budgeted amounts:
Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead ÷ Budgeted Activity Level of Allocation Base

The most common allocation bases are direct labor hours, direct labor costs, or machine hours. The rate is set once per year and used consistently. When actual overhead incurred differs from overhead applied, the difference is an over- or under-applied overhead variance.
Absorption Costing vs Variable (Direct) Costing
Both methods treat variable manufacturing costs the same way — as product costs. The only difference is how fixed manufacturing overhead is handled:

r: Product cost — goes into inventory, expensed when sold
Period cost — expensed immediately when incurred

4-1.v: The only income difference between absorption and variable costing comes from fixed factory overhead treatment. If production equals sales, income is identical. If production > sales, absorption costing shows HIGHER income (some fixed OH stays in ending inventory). If production < sales, variable costing shows HIGHER income.
Income Statement Format Comparison
Sales − COGS (variable + fixed mfg costs of units sold) = Gross Profit − Period costs = Operating Income
Sales − Variable mfg costs of units sold = Manufacturing Contribution Margin − Variable nonmfg costs = Contribution Margin − ALL Fixed costs = Operating Income
Process Costing vs Job Order Costing

r: Many identical/similar units produced (assembly line, clothing factory)
Units are distinct and separately identifiable (law firm, construction project)
Manufacturing where process is similar across products

4-1.v: Normal costing is not used in a process costing environment because it is too difficult to track actual direct costs for each production run. Normal costing is used mainly in job order costing environments.

4-2.v: Overhead costs are production and operation costs that cannot be directly traced to a specific product or unit. The three main categories of production costs are: (1) Direct materials, (2) Direct labor, and (3) Manufacturing (factory) overhead. Direct materials and direct labor are easy to trace — overhead is not, so it must be allocated.

r: Cleaning supplies, small tools, machine lubricant — not identifiable with a specific product
Plant superintendent salary, janitorial services, quality control — not directly attributable to a specific job
Factory rent, electricity, utilities, depreciation on production equipment

4-2.v: Factory overhead and manufacturing overhead are interchangeable terms. Both refer to the same thing.

v: Variable overheads change in total as activity changes; per-unit cost stays constant. Examples: indirect materials, equipment maintenance
Fixed overheads do not change in total within the relevant range. Examples: factory rent, depreciation on production equipment, plant manager's salary
Mixed overheads have both fixed and variable components. Example: electricity with a fixed monthly fee plus a per-kilowatt charge

4-2.v: Two primary methods exist for allocating manufacturing overhead:

v: Traditional method: groups all overhead into one (or a few) cost pools and allocates using a single cost driver (direct labor hours or machine hours). Simple but can distort product costs if different products use resources very differently
Activity-Based Costing (ABC): uses multiple cost pools, each with its own cost driver that best reflects what causes that cost. More accurate but more expensive to implement

4-2.v: A company can allocate overhead using one rate for the whole plant (plant-wide) or separate rates for each department (departmental):

r: Less accurate — all products treated the same regardless of departments they pass through
More accurate — each department uses the base that best reflects its cost drivers

4-2.v: When process costing is used and products pass through multiple departments, departmental overhead allocation is essential so each department's overhead is applied to the units worked on in that department.
The rate is always calculated using BUDGETED amounts — never actual — because actual overhead may not be known until after period end:
Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead ÷ Budgeted Activity Level of Allocation Base

The rate is set at the beginning of the year and applied consistently. A reverse formula is also important: Predetermined Rate × Budgeted Activity Level = Budgeted Manufacturing Overhead. This is tested frequently on the exam.

Fixed overhead is applied to production as if it were a variable cost — per unit, based on the standard activity allowed. This is why fixed overhead variances exist even though total actual fixed overhead barely changes.

ABC allocates overhead based on cost drivers specific to each activity. The premise: activities consume resources; products consume activities. A cost driver measures what causes a cost to be incurred.

v: Resource consumption cost drivers: measure resources consumed by an activity (e.g., machine hours consumed by the setup activity)
Activity consumption cost drivers: measure the amount of an activity consumed by a cost object (e.g., number of setups consumed by a product)

4-2.v: ABC is more accurate than traditional allocation for companies with diverse products. However, it does not conform to GAAP in its pure form and requires adaptation for external reporting. It is valuable for internal decision-making — especially pricing, product mix, and customer profitability decisions.
ABC can be applied to both manufacturing and nonmanufacturing overheads, and to service businesses as well as manufacturers.

4-3.v: Variance Analysis — The Big Picture

Variance analysis compares actual results to budgeted (standard) amounts. Variances alert management to investigate where performance differed from plan. The principle of management by exception focuses attention on the largest variances first — those with the highest priority.

r: Actual Results vs Flexible Budget (same activity level) — isolates price/efficiency issues
Flexible Budget vs Static Budget — shows impact of selling MORE or FEWER units than planned
Actual Results vs Static (original) Budget — total variance = flexible budget variance + sales volume variance

4-3.v: Actual Results − Flexible Budget Amount
Flexible Budget Amount − Static Budget Amount
Direct Materials Variances

The total direct materials variance is broken into two components — a quantity (efficiency) variance and a price variance. Together they explain the full difference between what was actually spent and what should have been spent for the actual output.
(AP × AQ) − (SP × SQ) = Actual Cost − Standard Cost Allowed for Actual Output
(AQ − SQ) × SP — where AQ = actual qty used, SQ = standard qty allowed for actual output, SP = standard price
(AP − SP) × AQ — where AP = actual price paid, SP = standard price
Positive variance = Unfavorable (cost higher than expected). Negative variance = Favorable (cost lower than expected). The quantity and price variances always sum to the total variance.

Worked Example — Medina Co. footballs: Standard = 1 sq meter leather at $5.00/meter. Actual: produced 250 footballs using 290 meters at $4.50/meter.
• Total variance: ($4.50×290) − ($5.00×250) = $1,305 − $1,250 = $55 Unfavorable
• Quantity variance: (290−250) × $5.00 = $200 Unfavorable
• Price variance: ($4.50−$5.00) × 290 = −$145 Favorable
• Check: $200U − $145F = $55U ✓

v: Unfavorable DM quantity: low quality materials causing spoilage, poor product design, theft, abnormal waste, machinery problems
Favorable DM quantity: better quality materials requiring less waste, improved production process
Unfavorable DM price: market price increases, poor purchasing decisions
Favorable DM price: market price decreases, better purchasing research (but may signal lower quality materials)

4-3.v: Direct Labor Variances
Direct labor variances follow the same structure as materials variances. The total labor variance is split into a rate variance (price) and efficiency variance (quantity).
(AR × AH) − (SR × SH) = Actual Labor Cost − Standard Labor Cost Allowed for Actual Output
(AR − SR) × AH — compares actual wage rate to standard wage rate, applied to actual hours worked
(AH − SH) × SR — compares actual hours used to standard hours allowed for actual output, at the standard rate
For the exam, be able to solve for any unknown in the variance formula. If the question gives you the variance amount and two of the three components, use algebra to find the third.

v: Unfavorable DL rate: renegotiated higher union wages, using more skilled workers than planned, inaccurate rate projections
Favorable DL rate: paying lower wages (may signal less qualified workers, which can create an unfavorable efficiency variance)
Unfavorable DL efficiency: inexperienced workers, equipment breakdowns, poor material quality causing rework
Favorable DL efficiency: better-trained workers, more highly skilled labor than planned

4-3.v: Fixed Overhead Variances

Fixed overhead is unique: it is budgeted as a lump sum (doesn't change with volume) but applied to production as if it were variable — per unit, at the predetermined rate. This creates a two-part variance structure.
Actual Fixed OH Incurred − Standard Fixed OH Applied (rate × standard qty allowed for actual output)
The total fixed overhead variance splits into two sub-variances:
Actual Fixed OH − Budgeted Fixed OH — measures whether actual fixed costs differed from budget
Budgeted Fixed OH − Fixed OH Applied — measures capacity utilization: did we produce more or fewer units than we planned when we set the rate?
There is NO fixed overhead efficiency variance. Fixed costs do not relate to output levels, so they cannot be used efficiently or inefficiently.

r: Actual fixed costs < budget (e.g. rent decrease)
Actual fixed costs > budget (e.g. new equipment purchase increases depreciation)
Actual production > budgeted production (more units = more overhead applied)
Actual production < budgeted production (less units = less overhead applied, fixed costs under-recovered

4-3.v:
Diagram showing how Total Fixed OH Variance is split:
  Actual Fixed OH Incurred
  − Budgeted Fixed OH              = Spending/Flexible Budget Variance
  Budgeted Fixed OH
  − Fixed OH Applied               = Production-Volume Variance
  Both together                    = Total Fixed OH Variance
The fixed overhead production-volume variance is NOT a comparison of actual to budgeted costs — it has no connection to actually incurred costs. It measures capacity utilization only.

4-4.v: The 80-20 rule: approximately 80% of a company's profits come from 20% of its customers — and 20% of customers are often completely unprofitable. Customer profitability analysis determines the profitability of each individual customer or group, enabling managers to:

v: Re-price activities that generate high costs-to-serve for certain customers
Offer discounts to low-cost-to-serve customers to increase their volume
Shift unprofitable customers toward higher-margin products/services
Target marketing efforts toward the most valuable customer types

4-4.v: Product profitability analysis works the same way — identifying which products or services are unprofitable so they can be re-priced, discontinued, or replaced. Accurate cost allocation is essential for both analyses.
Performance Measurement: ROI vs Residual Income
Return on Investment (ROI) and Residual Income (RI) are the two primary financial measures for evaluating the performance of a business segment (division, department, or investment center).
Income of Business Unit ÷ Assets of Business Unit

ROI measures the percentage return earned on assets invested. It can be used to compare segments against each other, against the whole company, or against a hurdle rate for project decisions. If a proposed project's ROI is lower than the segment's current ROI, a division manager will likely reject it — even if it exceeds the required rate of return. This is a key weakness of ROI.
Residual Income overcomes the key weakness of ROI by measuring the absolute dollar amount of return above the required rate of return:
Operating Income of Business Unit − (Assets of Business Unit × Required Rate of Return)

Worked Example — Medina Division: Assets = $4,000,000, Operating Income = $600,000, Required Rate = 10%
• Target return = $4,000,000 × 10% = $400,000
• RI = $600,000 − $400,000 = $200,000 (positive = good, earned more than required)
RI can be negative. Negative RI means the division earned less than its required rate of return — not necessarily a loss, but below what management requires.

r: Percentage — easy to compare across segments of different sizes
Managers may reject profitable investments that would lower their segment's ROI
Encourages accepting any project that earns more than the required rate — aligns with company goals
Absolute dollar amount — harder to compare segments of different sizes

4-4.v: If a project's expected ROI is greater than the required rate of return, RI will be positive and the project should be accepted. If the project's ROI is lower than the current segment ROI but higher than the required rate, ROI rejects it but RI accepts it — RI gives the correct decision.
Performance measurement is most meaningful when managers are held responsible only for what they can control. Responsibility centers define the scope of a manager's accountability:

Only costs that a manager can actually influence should be used to evaluate that manager's performance. Allocated costs (like centrally-incurred overheads) should NOT be used to evaluate segment managers because they cannot control them.

4-5.v: Cost-Volume-Profit (CVP) Analysis

CVP analysis (also called breakeven analysis) examines the relationship among revenue, costs, and profit. It is used for short-run decisions where prices and costs are relatively fixed, and profitability depends mainly on the quantity sold. CVP enables managers to find the level of sales needed to break even or achieve a target profit.

v: All costs are either fixed or variable (no mixed costs in the model)
Total costs and revenues are linear within the relevant range
Fixed costs remain constant over the relevant range
Unit variable costs remain constant — total variable costs change proportionally with volume
Selling price per unit remains constant
When a company sells multiple products, the sales mix remains constant

4-5.v: Contribution Margin

Contribution margin is the amount each unit sold contributes toward covering fixed costs and generating profit. Once fixed costs are fully covered, each additional unit sold contributes directly to operating income.
Selling Price per Unit − Variable Costs per Unit
Unit Contribution Margin ÷ Selling Price per Unit (expressed as a %)
The contribution margin income statement format separates variable and fixed costs to clearly show contribution margin — unlike the traditional absorption income statement which shows gross profit.
Breakeven Analysis
The breakeven point is where total revenue equals total costs (zero profit). It can be calculated in units or in sales revenue:
Total Fixed Costs ÷ Unit Contribution Margin
Total Fixed Costs ÷ Contribution Margin Ratio
(Fixed Costs + Target Profit) ÷ Unit Contribution Margin

Worked Example — Ray Company: Selling price $60, variable cost $35/unit, fixed costs $150,000.
• Unit CM = $60 − $35 = $25
• Breakeven = $150,000 ÷ $25 = 6,000 units
• Verification: Revenue $360,000 − Variable costs $210,000 − Fixed costs $150,000 = $0 ✓
Marginal Analysis
Marginal analysis examines how costs and benefits change with incremental changes in activity. Rational decision-makers act only when the marginal benefit exceeds the marginal cost of doing so.

v: Marginal revenue: the additional total revenue from increasing activity by one unit
Marginal cost: the additional total cost incurred from increasing activity by one unit
A rational company continues expanding production as long as marginal revenue > marginal cost

4-5.v: Relevant vs Non-Relevant Information
The primary challenge in decision-making is distinguishing relevant information from irrelevant information. A cost or revenue is relevant if and only if it meets BOTH criteria:

v: It occurs in the FUTURE (not the past)
It DIFFERS between the alternatives being considered

r: Already spent — cannot be recovered regardless of decision
Future costs that are the same for all alternatives
Future and differ between alternatives (choosing one option forecloses another)
Will go away if a particular option is chosen
Will continue regardless of which option is chosen

4-5.v: Sunk costs are never relevant. Don't let past spending influence future decisions — that is the sunk cost fallacy. Example: the original cost of equipment is irrelevant to a replace-or-keep decision.
Opportunity Costs

An opportunity cost is the benefit foregone from the best alternative use of a resource. It is an implicit (imputed) cost — it does not appear in accounting records, but it is a real economic cost and is always relevant to decisions.

Opportunity costs exist only when a resource is limited or constrained. If a company has unused production capacity, accepting a new order has zero opportunity cost. But if already at capacity, accepting the new order means giving up the contribution margin from other orders — that surrendered contribution margin is an opportunity cost.
Avoidable vs Unavoidable Costs

r: Will go away if a particular option is chosen
Will continue regardless of which option is chosen (e.g., non-cancelable lease)

4-5.v: Example — Outsourcing decision: If production is outsourced, variable manufacturing costs and some fixed costs (avoidable) go away — these are relevant. A non-cancelable equipment lease (unavoidable) continues whether production is in-house or outsourced — not relevant to the decision.

When a division is being considered for closure: the only costs relevant to the decision are the ones that would actually go away if the division closed. Centrally-allocated administrative costs are unavoidable and stay with the company even if the division closes — they are NOT relevant to the closure decision.
